findings.description: Classic N+1 in low_stock_report(): one COUNT(*) query against movements per low-stock product inside the loop (5,000 low-stock SKUs = 5,001 round trips); replace with a single LEFT JOIN ... GROUP BY or correlated-subquery aggregate.

Unbounded SELECT of the entire products table via fetchall() with the stock <= reorder_level predicate applied in Python — full-table transfer and in-memory materialization; push the filter into SQL (WHERE stock <= reorder_level).

Leading-wildcard LIKE '%fragment%' is non-sargable (full table scan per search) and the query has no LIMIT, so a short or empty fragment materializes the whole catalog as Product objects; add LIMIT/pagination and consider SQLite FTS if search volume matters.

Each per-SKU COUNT filters movements on sku and created_at; without a composite index movements(sku, created_at) every loop iteration is a full scan of the movements table — schema not in scope, verify the index exists.

A fresh sqlite3 connection is opened and torn down on every method call, discarding the per-connection prepared-statement cache and any PRAGMA tuning; in a per-record pipeline loop the connection churn rivals the query cost — reuse a long-lived connection or pool per thread.
fragment is f-string-interpolated into the LIKE clause — SQL injection (and breaks on quotes); use parameterized LIKE ? with escaped wildcards — detail deferred to critic-security.

Check-then-act race in reserve(): SELECT stock then UPDATE in separate statements allows overselling under concurrent calls; atomic 'UPDATE products SET stock = stock - ? WHERE sku = ? AND stock >= ?' with rowcount check fixes it (and halves round trips) — detail deferred to critic-logic.
Off-by-one: 'stock > quantity' refuses a reservation that exactly exhausts remaining stock (should be >=) — deferred to critic-logic.
